title: Liberty Wall Art from Scraps
summary: A rustic pieced-wood wall panel that spells or forms a design from mixed-width scrap boards on a plywood backer. Zero-precision, high-character — the imperfection is the point.
description:
This is decorative wall art assembled from a pile of scrap furring strips — mixed 1x2, 1x3, 1x4, and a 1x6 — glued and nailed to a plywood backer to form a pieced, planked pattern (the original spells out a patriotic 'Liberty' motif, but the technique makes any blocky word or geometric design). It's the ideal use for the offcut bin, and it's forgiving in a way almost nothing else is: the whole appeal is the handmade, slightly-uneven character, so precision genuinely doesn't matter.

The method is three steps and no joinery. Lay out the mixed-width boards into your pattern following a plan (or your own design), keeping in mind that the different board widths create the visual texture. Paint and distress the boards before assembly — painting them loose is far easier than cutting in around them once they're mounted. Then glue and nail them to the plywood backer, working from one corner across, keeping top edges flush and checking for square as you go so the overall panel stays rectangular even if individual boards don't line up perfectly.

The plywood back is cut slightly smaller than the board field so it doesn't show around the edges. Use a 1/4" to 1/2" plywood or even OSB — it's just a substrate. Because it's a decorative piece and not structural, 1" nails and glue are all it takes. Distress to taste; the more casual, the better it reads.

tools.note: Or any saw for the short crosscuts — a miter saw is faster if you have one.
1" nails, hand-driven, are all this needs.

materials.name: Plywood or OSB, 1/4–1/2 in, 4x4 sheet
materials.note: Backer — cut slightly smaller than the board field.

steps.title: Lay out the pattern
steps.body: Lay out the mixed-width boards into your design — the varied widths (1x4, 1x2, 1x3, and the single 1x6) are what create the visual texture. Follow a plan or improvise your own word or geometric motif. Don't stress about perfectly even boards; the handmade character is the whole appeal of the piece.

steps.title: Paint and distress the boards
steps.body: Paint your boards and distress them before assembly. Painting the pieces loose is far easier than trying to cut in around them once they're mounted, and distressing reads better when you can get at every edge.

steps.title: Glue and nail to the backer
steps.body: With glue and 1" nails, start at one corner and work across, fastening boards to the plywood backer. Keep the top edges flush and check for square as you go so the overall panel stays rectangular. The plywood back is cut slightly smaller than the field of boards so it doesn't peek out around the edges of the finished piece.

images.alt: Rustic pieced-wood wall art made from distressed scrap boards
